records.ety: [L. lapsus, fr. labi, p. p. lapsus, to slide, to fall: cf. F. laps. See Sleep.]

defs: A gliding, slipping, or gradual falling; an unobserved or imperceptible progress or passing away,; -- restricted usually to immaterial things, or to figurative uses.
quotes: The lapse to indolence is soft and imperceptible.
Bacon was content to wait the lapse of long centuries for his expected revenue of fame.

defs: A slip; an error; a fault; a failing in duty; a slight deviation from truth or rectitude.
quotes: To guard against those lapses and failings to which our infirmities daily expose us.

defs: The termination of a right or privilege through neglect to exercise it within the limited time, or through failure of some contingency; hence, the devolution of a right or privilege.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Lapsed (#); p. pr. & vb. n. Lapsing.]

defs: To pass slowly and smoothly downward, backward, or away; to slip downward, backward, or away; to glide; -- mostly restricted to figurative uses.
quotes: A tendency to lapse into the barbarity of those northern nations from whom we are descended.
Homer, in his characters of Vulcan and Thersites, has lapsed into the burlesque character.

defs: To slide or slip in moral conduct; to fail in duty; to fall from virtue; to deviate from rectitude; to commit a fault by inadvertence or mistake.
quotes: To lapse in fullness Is sorer than to lie for need.

defs: To fall or pass from one proprietor to another, or from the original destination, by the omission, negligence, or failure of some one, as a patron, a legatee, etc.
To become ineffectual or void; to fall.
quotes: If the archbishop shall not fill it up within six months ensuing, it lapses to the king.

defs: To let slip; to permit to devolve on another; to allow to pass.
quotes: An appeal may be deserted by the appellant's lapsing the term of law.

defs: To surprise in a fault or error; hence, to surprise or catch, as an offender.
quotes: For which, if be lapsed in this place, I shall pay dear.
